strands.title: Core Projection Pipeline Architecture
strands.content: The hamutay system implements a three-stage pipeline: log_reader (ConversationTurn serialization from session JSONL) -> projector (Projector.project() method that maintains state across turns and emits Tensor objects) -> observation/output (run_projection.py orchestrates the full cycle). The Projector maintains internal cycle state and accumulates tensors across a conversation trajectory. New evidence: run_projection.py file successfully created at /home/tony/projects/hamutay/src/hamutay/run_projection.py. The workflow has progressed from architectural design to concrete implementation scaffolding.

key_claims.text: The hamutay pipeline has three stages: log_reader, projector, and observation/output orchestration
run_projection.py has been successfully created as a concrete harness for the experiment
The projector maintains internal cycle state and accumulates tensors across conversation trajectory
ConversationTurn objects are serialized from session JSONL files as input to the pipeline
Session files exist at /home/tony/projects/pichay/experiments/baseline_run1/session/ with actual conversation message content

strands.title: Projector State Management and Tensor Evolution
strands.content: The projector maintains internal state (cycle counter) across multiple turn projections. Each call to project() produces a new Tensor object that integrates prior state. The trajectory reveals tensor evolution: strand counts, claim accumulation, loss declarations, open question persistence. Epistemic values (truth, indeterminacy, falsity) are tracked per tensor and can be analyzed for stability or drift across cycles. Cycle 48 shows the experiment transitioning from preparation to data loading — the assistant is now actively checking whether session JSONL files contain actual conversation content (not just proxy metadata). This is a critical validation step before projection can proceed.

key_claims.text: The Projector.project() method must integrate prior tensor state to produce accumulated semantic structure
Tensor objects carry forward open questions unless explicitly resolved in new turns
Epistemic values per strand must track truth, indeterminacy, and falsity as separate dimensions
Tensor evolution across cycles can be analyzed post-hoc from persisted JSON files for stability metrics

strands.title: Strand Stability and Semantic Thread Evolution
strands.content: The print_tensor_trajectory() function analyzes strand evolution across the tensor sequence. Strands have titles; the trajectory tracks which strands are added, removed, or remain stable between consecutive cycles. This enables measurement of semantic coherence — whether key themes converge, fragment, or persist as more conversational content is processed. The current cycle has not yet executed print_tensor_trajectory(), but the infrastructure for observing strand stability is in place. Five strands are maintained from cycle 47, indicating semantic structure persists and is ready to integrate new data.

key_claims.text: Strands with consistent titles across cycles indicate stable semantic themes
print_tensor_trajectory() enables post-hoc analysis of strand convergence or fragmentation patterns
New strands may be created when conversation content introduces semantically novel topics

strands.title: Loss Declaration as Explicit Information Accounting
strands.content: Each tensor includes declared_losses array tracking what information was dropped during projection and why. The prior tensor declared five critical losses (system architecture detail, feedback loops, sample data, model invocation details, strand creation rules). Cycle 48 continues to face the same practical constraints — the Projector implementation details remain opaque, and until actual execution occurs or implementation code is revealed, these losses will persist. However, the forward progression (file creation success, data loading attempt) suggests the system is becoming less theoretical and more observational.

key_claims.text: Declared losses from prior cycles persist until the underlying information gaps are filled
Loss declarations are explicit audit points showing where compression occurred and why
Practical constraints (missing implementation, opaque architecture) are a primary loss category affecting projection quality

strands.title: Experimental Setup Validation and Data Access
strands.content: Cycle 48 marks a critical transition: from architectural specification to validation. The assistant successfully created run_projection.py and is now attempting to read actual session JSONL data from /home/tony/projects/pichay/experiments/baseline_run1/session/. The read operation targets a specific session UUID (d5da3c7a-3c3c-4047-a4ee-276be57567c1.jsonl) with a limit of 3 turns. This is the first concrete test of whether the data infrastructure exists and is accessible. If the read succeeds, it will provide actual ConversationTurn objects with real message content — the raw material for tensor projection. Success here would resolve the 'no concrete output data' loss declared in cycle 47.

key_claims.text: run_projection.py has been successfully instantiated as a working harness
Session data is being loaded from /home/tony/projects/pichay/experiments/baseline_run1/session/ directory
The read operation is checking whether session JSONL contains actual conversation message content (not just metadata)
A successful session data read would resolve the loss category 'no concrete output data' from cycle 47

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Complete Projector class implementation and project() method logic
declared_losses.why: run_projection.py successfully created but the actual Projector.project() method, cycle state management, and tensor construction logic remain unseen

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Tensor class definition, schema, and model_dump_json() behavior
declared_losses.why: Code assumes Tensor structure but full model definition, field defaults, and serialization behavior not yet provided

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Actual session JSONL read result and ConversationTurn object content
declared_losses.why: Read operation at cycle 48 is in progress (tool_use: Read); the actual content of the first 3 turns from session d5da3c7a-3c3c-4047-a4ee-276be57567c1.jsonl not yet delivered

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Model invocation details and LLM prompt engineering in Projector
declared_losses.why: Model parameter passed to Projector but no detail on how/when LLM is called, what prompts drive strand creation, or how raw output becomes Tensor structure

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Definition of strand title semantics and creation rules
declared_losses.why: Five strands maintained but no explicit grammar or logic for strand naming, creation, or merging heuristics

open_questions: What is the complete implementation of Projector.project() and how does it transition from one Tensor to the next?
How are strands identified, named, and merged? Is there a strand creation grammar or heuristic?
What prompts or logic does the Projector use to populate strands, key_claims, declared_losses, and open_questions from raw conversation content?
How does Projector maintain prior tensor state when projecting the next turn — is it stored in memory, embedded in a system prompt, or reconstructed from persistent JSON?
What does Tensor.token_estimate() count, and how does it relate to actual LLM token consumption?
How are epistemic values (truth, indeterminacy, falsity) computed per strand or per tensor — is there an aggregation function?
What is the expected behavior when run_projection processes a session with dozens or hundreds of turns — does the tensor size grow linearly or stabilize?
How will the system handle conversation turns with missing or incomplete metadata (as mentioned in prior open questions)?
What validation ensures that Tensor objects conform to the expected schema before serialization to JSON?
Will the session JSONL read succeed? If so, what is the structure and content of the ConversationTurn objects returned?
Once session data is loaded, what are the specific strand titles and epistemic patterns that emerge in the first few projections?
instructions_for_next:
[
  "If session JSONL read returns successfully: extract ConversationTurn structure, message content, and metadata; identify what fields are populated vs. missing; assess data quality for projection",
  "If read fails: escalate to declared_losses, document the error, and recommend fallback strategies (synthetic data, log inspection, schema validation)",
  "If Projector implementation or LLM invocation details appear: extract the project() method signature, cycle state feeding mechanism, prompt template(s), and epistemic value computation logic",
  "When first concrete tensor is emitted (from run_projection.py): extract actual strand titles, key_claim counts, loss declarations seen in practice, and compare to prior tensor schema",
  "Monitor for tensor evolution patterns: measure strand count stability, epistemic value drift, loss category frequency, and open question resolution rate across consecutive cycles",
  "If the system runs successfully on real data: analyze whether tensor converges, diverges, or oscillates; extract actual token counts, compression ratios, and wall-clock performance",
  "Watch for edge cases: how does projector handle empty turns, metadata-only turns, very long turns, or turns with schema violations?",
  "If issues arise (API errors, schema mismatches, data format problems): immediately declare them in losses and update open_questions with failure mode analysis"
]
